description: This figure presents the strain hardening rate (dσ/dε) versus true strain curves for Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC low-density steels with three different carbon contents: 0.5C (black curve), 0.8C (red curve), and 1.2C (green curve). The plot reveals distinctly different strain hardening behaviors among the three alloys. Both 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibit a two-stage strain hardening characteristic with continuously decreasing strain hardening rates as strain increases, though 0.8C maintains higher strain hardening values than 0.5C throughout most of the deformation process. In contrast, the 1.2C steel displays a unique three-stage strain hardening behavior: an initial rapid decrease followed by an increasing stage reaching a plateau around 1900 MPa between 0.15–0.35 strain, and finally a sharp drop at high strains. The 1.2C steel sustains sufficient strain hardening capacity beyond 0.026 strain to prevent strain localization, correlating with its superior ductility (56.2% elongation) compared to 0.5C (32.8%) and 0.8C (38.4%) steels. This variation is attributed to microstructural differences: 0.5C has a duplex ferritic-austenitic structure without precipitates, 0.8C contains ordered phases (B2, DO3) in ferrite and κ carbides in austenite, while 1.2C is fully austenitic with uniformly distributed κ carbides, leading to different dislocation evolution mechanisms (planar slip, Taylor lattice, microbands) during tensile deformation.
